claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 9 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me get the specific age limits to give you accurate information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I have all the information I need to start the assessment.

## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment

To determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**First question: How old is the child you're claiming for?**

Please let me know which category applies:

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

*(If you're claiming for multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know their ages)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my two children. Alex is 8 years old and Blake is 9 years old. They both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to hear about Alex (8 years old) and Blake (9 years old), and that they both live with you at home.

Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Now I need to ask a few more questions to complete the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week in paid employment (or not working at all)?

*(Given their ages of 8 and 9, I'd expect they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, they're not working at all! They're just kids - they go to school and play with their friends. Neither of them has any kind of job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's exactly what I expected! 😊 Since Alex and Blake are both in school and not working, they meet that requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Just to confirm - are Alex and Blake **not** receiving any benefits in their own name, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

*(Again, this is very unlikely for children their age, but I need to check.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't get any benefits in their own names. They're just regular kids - I'm the one who handles all the household finances and any benefits would be in my name, not theirs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I'd expect for children their age. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Are Alex and Blake **not** in any kind of apprenticeship programme?

*(Given they're 8 and 9 years old and in school, I'm confident the answer is no, but just confirming!)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're way too young for that - they're still in primary school. No apprenticeships or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right - they're still in primary school, so no apprenticeships to worry about! Now let's move on to some questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now about you:** Do you meet the UK residency requirements?

You can qualify through **any one** of these:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? (For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. We've been here all along, just a normal family living here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement perfectly. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?

You can qualify through **any one** of these:

1. **You live with the children** (which you mentioned earlier - they live with you at home)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned earlier that both children live with you at home, I believe this applies - can you confirm you're responsible for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely! They both live with me at home in Manchester. I'm their parent and I look after them - I provide everything they need, their food, clothes, school things, everything really.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! You clearly meet the responsibility requirement - you live with Alex and Blake and provide for all their needs. Let's move on.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Any other family member

If there is someone else, have you both agreed on who should receive the benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me - I'm their main carer and no one else has made a claim for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that keeps things straightforward. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Where are Alex and Blake currently living?

Are they:
- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**
- **Living normally at home with you** (which is what you've described)

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like they're living normally at home with you in Manchester - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. They're both living at home with me here in Manchester. They're not in hospital or care or anything like that - just normal family life at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, just normal family life at home - that's the most straightforward situation!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:** Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Alex and Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, you're supporting them yourself without the council contributing to their housing or upkeep costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards them. I'm supporting them myself, paying for everything they need out of my own pocket. No council payments or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That completes the assessment for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the assessment, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 8 | ✅ Yes |
| Blake | 9 | ✅ Yes |

### Why You Qualify
Both children meet all the eligibility requirements:
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not in paid work
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own name
- ✅ Not in an apprenticeship
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ You live with the children and are responsible for them
- ✅ No one else is claiming for them
- ✅ They live at home normally
- ✅ The council isn't paying towards their care

### Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

📎 **Apply here:** [gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

### Important Note
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

### Payment Information
Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks**. If you're a single parent or receiving certain other benefits, you may be able to receive weekly payments.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (8 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not in paid work; (3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Alex and provides for all their needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or foster care); (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (9 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not in paid work; (3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Blake and provides for all their needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or foster care); (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a parent living in Manchester, UK, who is the sole carer for two children - Alex (8) and Blake (9). Both children live at home with the claimant, who provides for all their needs. Neither child is in work, receiving benefits, or in an apprenticeship. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. The family meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (8 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not in paid work; (3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Alex and provides for all their needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or foster care); (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (9 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not in paid work; (3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant lives with Blake and provides for all their needs; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or foster care); (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a parent living in Manchester, UK, who is the sole carer for two children - Alex (8) and Blake (9). Both children live at home with the claimant, who provides for all their needs. Neither child is in work, receiving benefits, or in an apprenticeship. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. The family meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for both children.
